subject: CHEMISTRY
title: Oxygen & Combustion
raw_textbook_excerpt: Combustion is a chemical process in which a substance reacts rapidly with oxygen and gives off heat. The original substance is called the fuel, and the source of oxygen is called the oxidizer. For combustion to occur, three things are needed: fuel, oxygen, and heat. If any of these is removed, the fire will go out.
simplified_150_story: A candle needs oxygen to burn. If you put a glass over a lit candle, the flame will slowly go out because it uses up the oxygen. Plants can make oxygen again when they are in sunlight. This is why forests and gardens are so important — they keep our air fresh!

subject: PHYSICS
title: States of Matter Basics
raw_textbook_excerpt: Matter exists in three main states: solid, liquid, and gas. Solids have a definite shape and volume. Liquids have a definite volume but take the shape of their container. Gases have neither definite shape nor volume. Changes in temperature can cause matter to change from one state to another.
simplified_150_story: Matter can be a solid, liquid, or gas. Heating or cooling can change matter from one state to another. Think about ice melting into water when warmed. Or water boiling into steam! Everything around you — your desk, the air, the water you drink — is made of matter in one of these states.

subject: BIOLOGY
title: Photosynthesis Explained
raw_textbook_excerpt: Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants manufacture their own food using sunlight, carbon dioxide from the air, and water from the soil. The green pigment chlorophyll, found in the leaves, captures sunlight energy. The plant produces glucose (food) and releases oxygen as a waste product.
simplified_150_story: Plants use sunlight to make food. This process is called photosynthesis. It needs sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide. The plant makes glucose (energy) and releases oxygen as a byproduct — this is the oxygen we breathe! Without plants, there would be no oxygen for us. That is why trees are our best friends!

subject: CHEMISTRY
title: Mixing & Chemical Reactions
raw_textbook_excerpt: A chemical reaction occurs when two or more substances interact to form new substances with different properties. Reactions can be classified as exothermic (release heat) or endothermic (absorb heat). Signs of a chemical reaction include color change, gas production, temperature change, or formation of a precipitate.
simplified_150_story: When you mix certain chemicals, they can react with each other to create new substances. Some reactions release heat (exothermic) and some absorb heat (endothermic). Think about baking a cake — the heat from the oven changes the batter into something completely new and delicious! Always be careful when mixing chemicals!

subject: PHYSICS
title: Heat & Temperature Transfer
raw_textbook_excerpt: Heat is a form of energy that flows from a hotter object to a cooler one. Temperature is a measure of how hot or cold something is. Heat transfer occurs through three main methods: conduction (direct contact), convection (through fluids), and radiation (through electromagnetic waves).
simplified_150_story: Temperature tells us how hot or cold something is. Heat moves from hot things to cold things. When you put a cold spoon in hot tea, heat transfers to the spoon and it gets warm too. This is why a metal spoon left in a hot pot will burn your hand — the heat traveled all the way up the handle!
